claimed_contribution: Proposes an ability-based relational processing advantage for high-interdependence consumers, defined as a wider and deeper search for relationships between a parent brand and an extension.

Shows that this advantage is contingent on processing motivation, which helps explain when self-construal differences in extension fit and evaluation emerge across close, moderate, and far brand stretches.
Demonstrates managerially relevant ways to harness the effect for far stretches (for example, motivation-inducing ad copy) and provides process evidence via thought-listing and mediation tests.
context: The paper studies consumer evaluations of hypothetical brand extensions in branded product markets, using familiar parent brands such as Johnson & Johnson, Sony, and Adidas. The substantive setting is brand stretch decisions from close to far extensions under typical low-to-moderate involvement conditions.
design_and_data: The author conducted three pretests to calibrate extension stretch and map association similarity for candidate extensions (n=53, n=18, n=50). Five between-subjects student-sample experiments then tested the framework: Experiment 1 (n=246; measured interdependence x 7 extension replicates), Experiment 2 (n=127; primed self-construal x low/moderate/high stretch), Experiment 3 (n=85; measured interdependence x motivation instructions on a far extension), Experiment 4 (n=86; measured interdependence x ad headline question versus statement on a far extension), and Experiment 5 (n=79; measured interdependence x relationship generated versus given). Key outcomes were perceived fit, extension evaluation, purchase or information-search intentions, and coded thought listings; brand familiarity was used as a covariate.
main_findings: Across Experiments 1 and 2, high-interdependence or interdependence-primed participants showed higher perceived fit and more favorable responses than comparison groups for moderate-stretch extensions, but not for close or far stretches under baseline conditions.

For far extensions, increasing processing motivation (via task instructions or question-based ad headlines) increased high interdependents' relational thoughts, perceived fit, and extension acceptance, while low interdependents showed little or no comparable gain.

Experiment 5 found that high interdependents generated more relational links when asked to do so, and explicitly providing relational links increased low interdependents' fit and evaluations, supporting an ability-based mechanism.
one_line_summary: This paper shows that interdependent self-construal can increase acceptance of brand stretches by raising perceived fit through relational processing, but mainly when consumers are motivated to elaborate.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior brand extension and self-construal research by linking interdependent self-views to a relational processing mechanism and by showing that its effect on extension fit is motivation contingent.
research_gap: Prior self-construal studies on relational grouping largely used tasks that explicitly instructed participants to generate relationships and stimuli with available associations. The paper argues it was unclear whether interdependents would still show a relational advantage for low-relational, far brand extensions in typical low-involvement marketplace settings.
research_question: How does consumer self-construal affect how far a brand can stretch through perceived fit and extension acceptance? Specifically, do interdependent consumers possess a relational processing advantage in connecting extensions to parent brands, and under what motivation and stretch conditions does this advantage emerge?
